text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Eleanor Hart  
St. Augustine’s Neurology Centre  
Level 3, Neurology Outpatients, St. Augustine’s Hospital, Bristol BS2 8HL  

Dear Dr Eleanor Hart,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-02-1990, Hospital No: A937514 NHS No. 9057316428  
Flat 2, 18 River View, Bristol, BS1 4QX 

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; aetiology not established. Prior normal MRI brain (2023). Ambulatory EEG (2024) non-diagnostic.

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily  
Midazolam 10 mg buccal prefilled device for rescue (patient-held)

Plan: 

- Continue Levetiracetam at current dose.  
- Maintain access to buccal midazolam at work and home; ensure devices are within expiry and accessible despite cold-chain PPE.  
- Occupational advice provided regarding rescue device storage outside deep-freeze zones and training for designated colleagues.  
- No driving as per DVLA guidance only if future events occur; currently meets criteria given No events for twelve months.  
- Routine bloods (U&Es, FBC, LFTs) in 6 months.  
- Follow up in 9–12 months, earlier if breakthrough events or adverse effects.

I reviewed Michael Turner in my clinic today. He works in a cold-chain warehouse where sub-zero temperatures and mandatory insulated gloves can slow handling of rescue devices. He reports improved sleep regularity and reduced overtime on night shifts. He has had No events for twelve months, with no episodes of loss of awareness, injuries, or postictal confusion. There are no mood or cognitive complaints on Levetiracetam; no irritability reported by him or his partner.

We discussed practicalities at work: keeping a buccal midazolam device in a belt pouch when in ambient-temperature areas, with a second device stored in the site first-aid room at room temperature to avoid viscosity issues in the cold. He confirms that two supervisors have been shown how to administer the rescue medication and that the workplace incident plan has been updated. I advised checking device expiry dates monthly and replacing any device exposed to freezing conditions. These measures are patient-reported and coordinated with his employer’s safety team.

I have not altered his antiseizure medication. Given the stable period — No events for twelve months — we agreed to continue the current regimen and review in 9–12 months. He will contact us sooner if there are any breakthrough events, intolerable side effects, or concerns about rescue access during shifts.

With best wishes,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states multiple times that the patient has had no events for twelve months. 'Events' in this context clearly refers to seizures, as the diagnoses and rescue medication discussion concern seizures. The DVLA driving note also ties to seizure-free status. Therefore, the patient is currently seizure free and has remained so for at least 12 months, satisfying the seizure-free criterion (>6 months). No other seizure frequencies are provided that would be higher. Final label: seizure free for 12 month.
